I remember the rankings: riding distance was 31st out of 100, money earned also 31st, and orders received 31st too. Hmm, so all three metrics are in the middle range. That means I'm not doing badly, but there's definitely room for improvement. 

I wonder why my earnings and order counts aren't higher. Maybe it has to do with the hours I work. Working from 8 AM to 6 PM is a solid 10-hour shift. But perhaps during those times, traffic was heavy or there were fewer orders in the areas I covered. Or maybe I didn't optimize my routes as well as I could have.

Since all three metrics are around the same rank, it's possible that my efficiency could be better. Maybe if I adjust my working hours to peak times when more orders come in, I can increase both the number of deliveries and earnings without necessarily increasing the distance I ride. 

What are the peak times? Typically, lunchtime is a big one—around 12 PM to 2 PM—and dinner time from 6 PM to 8 PM. But since I finished at 6 PM yesterday, maybe extending my shift into the early evening could help capture more orders.

Alternatively, starting earlier might allow me to cover areas with higher demand in the morning before others arrive. Or perhaps there's a slower period in the middle of the day where adjusting my hours can let me take breaks when it's less busy.

Wait, but I also need to consider how long I'm working. If I start later and finish later, maybe that gives me more time during the busier periods without overlapping too much with rush hour traffic. 

Let me think about possible shifts. If I start at 9 AM instead of 8 AM, perhaps avoiding some early morning traffic, but then ending at 7 PM could capture more evening orders. That's an 10-hour shift again, so similar in length but shifted later.

Alternatively, starting earlier at 7 AM and finishing at 5 PM might let me cover the morning rush better, but I'm not sure if that would increase my order count enough to justify the change.

I should also consider my physical well-being since I'm a fitness enthusiast. Working longer hours could be tiring, so finding a balance is important. Maybe starting later and finishing later could allow me to work more efficiently during peak times without getting too fatigued.

Another thought: perhaps working a shorter shift but during high demand could yield better results. For example, 10 AM to 6 PM might avoid some of the morning rush but still catch lunchtime and dinner orders. But I'm not sure if that's enough time to cover more ground.

Wait, maybe the issue is that by finishing at 6 PM yesterday, I missed out on the dinner peak. If I finish later, say 7 PM, I could potentially deliver more during that busy time. That might increase my order count and earnings without having to ride too much extra distance if orders are clustered in areas I already cover.

So, considering all this, shifting my start time a bit later but extending the end time into the evening peak seems like it could be beneficial. Let me test this idea: starting at 9 AM and finishing at 7 PM. That gives me 10 hours again, but with more coverage during the dinner rush.

Alternatively, maybe even pushing it to 8 AM start and 7 PM finish would give an extra hour in the evening, but that's a longer shift which might be tiring. 

Hmm, perhaps starting at 9 AM and ending at 7 PM is a good middle ground. It allows me to avoid some of the early morning traffic, work through lunchtime, and then into the dinner peak without overworking myself too much.

I should also think about how my riding distance was ranked similarly. If I'm covering more high-demand areas during busier times, maybe that would increase the number of orders without needing to ride as far. Or perhaps it's just a matter of being in the right place at the right time.

In conclusion, adjusting my working hours to start later and finish later might help me capture more orders and earn more money by aligning with peak demand periods.
